does: This paper proposes ReNeLLM, an automated framework for generating jailbreak prompts that circumvent LLM safety mechanisms. The method generalizes jailbreak attacks into two components: prompt rewriting, which reformulates harmful requests with different linguistic framing, and scenario nesting, which embeds requests within hypothetical or roleplay contexts. Rather than requiring manual design or optimization on white-box models, ReNeLLM leverages LLMs themselves to generate effective adversarial prompts automatically. Experiments demonstrate substantial improvements in attack success rates while reducing computational cost compared to existing jailbreak methods, and the work reveals vulnerabilities in current defense mechanisms.
connects: This work extends a growing line of research on adversarial robustness of large language models. It builds on prior jailbreak studies that identified safety alignment vulnerabilities through prompt engineering, including role-playing attacks and instruction-following exploits. The paper responds to limitations in existing approaches that either require time-consuming manual crafting of adversarial examples or depend on white-box optimization across multiple models. It connects to broader adversarial machine learning literature studying how neural models can be fooled through carefully constructed inputs, while addressing the specific challenge of LLM safety alignment, which has become critical as these models are deployed in high-stakes applications.
enables: This research enables more systematic evaluation of LLM vulnerabilities and informs the development of more robust safety mechanisms. By automating jailbreak generation, the framework makes it easier for AI safety researchers to stress-test defenses at scale and identify failure modes in alignment approaches. The insights about how prompt rewriting and scenario nesting bypass safeguards can guide the design of stronger defense strategies that account for linguistic variability and contextual embedding. This work points toward understanding fundamental limitations in instruction-following-based safety approaches and motivates research into alternative safety paradigms, such as constitutional AI or mechanistic interpretability of safety mechanisms.
